description: LoRA, PEFT, RLHF mechanics, DPO, instruction tuning, and domain adaptation.

questions.question: What is 'fine-tuning' a language model?
options: Adjusting the model's sampling temperature and top-p settings to improve response quality
Continuing to train a pre-trained model on a smaller, task-specific dataset to specialize its behavior
Compressing the model's weights to make inference faster on lower-powered hardware
Writing a detailed system prompt that instructs the model how to respond in a specific domain
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning takes a pre-trained model and trains it further on a narrower dataset — instruction pairs, domain documents, or preference data — to shift its behavior toward a desired task or style while retaining broad pre-training knowledge.

questions.question: What does 'PEFT' stand for, and why is it useful?
options: Post-Evaluation Fine-Tuning — adding extra training after benchmarking to fix low-scoring questions
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning — updating only a small fraction of parameters so fine-tuning is cheap and avoids catastrophic forgetting
Probabilistic Ensemble Fine-Tuning — combining multiple model checkpoints to reduce variance on downstream tasks
Pre-Encoded Feature Transfer — re-using early-layer feature maps across different model families without retraining
questions.explanation: PEFT methods (LoRA, prefix tuning, adapters) add or modify a tiny number of trainable parameters while keeping the bulk of the pre-trained weights frozen. This drastically reduces GPU memory and compute requirements and lets multiple fine-tuned variants share one base model.

questions.question: What is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)?
options: A library for locally running quantized open-weights models on consumer GPUs
A PEFT technique that injects small trainable low-rank matrices into the model's weight layers so only those extra parameters are updated during fine-tuning
A method for merging two fully fine-tuned model checkpoints by averaging their weight tensors
A sampling algorithm that restricts generation to a low-rank vocabulary subset to reduce hallucination
questions.explanation: LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) freezes the original weight matrix W and learns two small matrices A and B such that the fine-tuning update is ΔW = BA (a low-rank product). At inference, BA can be merged into W with zero overhead. QLoRA extends it with 4-bit quantization for even lower memory.

questions.question: What is 'instruction tuning'?
options: Writing a long system prompt with step-by-step instructions at the start of every API request
Fine-tuning a base language model on (instruction, response) pairs so it learns to follow natural-language directions
A data augmentation technique that rewrites training examples as instructions to reduce repetition
Updating only the instruction-following layers of a model while leaving all other layers frozen
questions.explanation: Instruction tuning (e.g., FLAN, InstructGPT) fine-tunes a base model on diverse (instruction, desired output) examples. It transforms a text-completion model into an assistant that reliably follows user directives across many task types.

questions.question: What is the role of the 'reward model' in RLHF?
options: It is a lookup table that maps predefined keywords in the model output to fixed numerical reward scores
It is a separate neural network trained on human preference rankings that predicts how much a human would prefer one model output over another
It is the main language model that is being fine-tuned; the term 'reward model' is another name for the policy model
It is a classifier that filters unsafe content from training batches before they are fed into the policy update step
questions.explanation: In RLHF, human annotators rank pairs of model outputs. A reward model (typically an LLM with a scalar head) is trained on those rankings to predict preference scores. The policy (the main LLM) is then optimized with PPO to maximize the reward model's scores, subject to a KL penalty against the reference policy.

questions.question: What is DPO (Direct Preference Optimization), and how does it differ from PPO-based RLHF?
options: DPO is a pre-training objective that directly optimizes for human preference data without any supervised fine-tuning phase
DPO reformulates the RLHF objective so the language model itself implicitly acts as its own reward model, removing the need for a separate reward model and a reinforcement-learning optimizer
DPO fine-tunes only the decoder layers while PPO fine-tunes all layers, making DPO faster but less effective
DPO and PPO are two interchangeable names for the same algorithm — one used by academic researchers and the other by industry practitioners
questions.explanation: DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023) shows the RLHF objective can be optimized directly as a classification loss over (chosen, rejected) pairs without training a reward model or running an RL loop. It is simpler, more stable, and widely adopted in open-source fine-tuning pipelines.

questions.question: What is 'catastrophic forgetting' in the context of fine-tuning?
options: The model discarding knowledge about the fine-tuning task when it encounters out-of-distribution prompts at test time
The tendency of neural networks to over-write previously learned general knowledge when trained on a new, narrow dataset
A failure mode where the model forgets the beginning of long prompts because they fall outside the attention window
A hardware fault causing GPU memory to be wiped when a fine-tuning job is interrupted mid-epoch
questions.explanation: When a model is fully fine-tuned on a new task, gradient updates can overwrite the weights encoding earlier capabilities — a phenomenon called catastrophic forgetting. PEFT methods (LoRA, adapters) mitigate it by keeping most pre-trained weights frozen.

questions.question: What is 'QLoRA' and what problem does it solve?
options: A quantization format that allows model weights to be served with query-level access control for multi-tenant deployments
A technique that combines 4-bit NF4 quantization of the frozen base model with LoRA adapters, dramatically cutting the GPU memory needed to fine-tune large models
A quality-assurance protocol that runs LoRA fine-tuned models through standardized benchmark suites before deployment
A variant of LoRA that constrains rank to powers of two so weight matrices can be stored in quarter-precision integers
questions.explanation: QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) quantizes the frozen base model to 4-bit NF4 format, adds LoRA adapters at full precision, and uses double quantization and paged optimizers to fit large-model fine-tuning on a single consumer GPU. It democratized fine-tuning of 7B–70B models.

questions.question: What is 'RLAIF' (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback), and what is the key motivation for using it instead of RLHF?
options: RLAIF replaces the language model policy with a smaller distilled model to reduce the cost of inference at scale
RLAIF uses a separate AI model to generate the preference labels used to train the reward model, reducing dependency on expensive human annotation while retaining alignment signal
RLAIF is an offline version of RLHF that pre-computes all reward scores before training begins, making the pipeline deterministic
RLAIF fine-tunes only the attention layers using synthetic preference data, while RLHF fine-tunes all layers using human data
questions.explanation: RLAIF (Lee et al., 2023; used in Anthropic's Constitutional AI) replaces human preference labelers with a capable LLM judge. The LLM scores response pairs according to a principle set, removing the bottleneck of expensive human annotation while achieving preference-learning quality comparable to RLHF at scale.

questions.question: What is 'model merging' (e.g., SLERP or TIES-merging), and why is it appealing compared to ensembling?
options: Merging the training datasets of two models and retraining from scratch to produce a combined model
Averaging the logits of two separately running models at inference time so predictions reflect both models' distributions

Combining the weight tensors of two or more fine-tuned models — e.g., via spherical interpolation or sign-based merging — to obtain a single model that inherits capabilities from each parent without any additional training or inference overhead
Splitting a large model into two halves that each handle different parts of a task and communicate via a learned routing layer
questions.explanation: Model merging operates directly in weight space: techniques like SLERP (spherical linear interpolation) or TIES (resolving sign conflicts before averaging) blend two fine-tuned checkpoints into one. The merged model inherits complementary skills with zero extra inference cost — unlike ensembling which requires running multiple models. It is widely used in the open-source community (e.g., Hugging Face mergekit).
